---
title: "Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction"
subtitle: "CPLN 5080 - Spring 2026"
author: "Shubhanga Satyal"
date: today
format:
  html:
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
editor: visual
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

This assignment recreates the Indego assessment from the in-class assignment and aligns it with the second-quarter data. It then develops a basic understanding of demand patterns in the second quarter and compares the model results. The assignment takes the analysis a step further by adding spatial features and testing potential improvements. Finally, it concludes with an equity-focused analysis to help inform policy-level decisions.

# Part 1: Replicate with Different Quarter

## Coding and Replicating

We selected Q2 2025 (April–June) for this homework because it represents Philadelphia's *prime biking season*: temperatures rise, daylight extends, and ridership typically surges relative to Q1's winter baseline. This makes Q2 a compelling contrast case, the same model framework applied to a completely different demand regime.

```{r setup}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(sf)
library(tigris)
library(tidycensus)
library(riem)  # For Philadelphia weather from ASOS stations

library(viridis) # color scales for visualization
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(patchwork)


library(here)
options(scipen = 999) # Get rid of scientific notation to make it look tidier

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)
options(tigris_progress = FALSE)

```

Defining reusable plot and map themes for consistent ggplot2 styling, plus a 5-color palette.

```{r themes}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# plotTheme: reusable ggplot2 theme for charts and graphs 
plotTheme1 <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x   = element_text(size = 10, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y   = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title    = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "#D0D0D0", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks    = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

# mapTheme: reusable ggplot2 theme for spatial/map plots 
mapTheme1 <- theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position  = "right",
    plot.margin      = margin(1, 1, 1, 1, "cm"),
    legend.key.height = unit(1, "cm"),
    legend.key.width  = unit(0.2, "cm")
  )

#palette5: 5-color palette for categorical or sequential mapping
palette51 <- c("lightpink", "red", "lightblue", "darkblue", "green")
```

Setting up census API key

```{r set-census-api-key}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

#setting up census API
census_api_key(Sys.getenv("82d6bed7434dbceef02889e5a0d354feff424c8b"))

```

Loading Indego data. As earlier mentioned we are using Quarter 2, which is April - June fopr this to follow the analysis from Quarter 1 to see whgat kind of changes occur. Additionally, we also check for the peak time for biking analysis since it is high in demand for spring.

```{r load_indego}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Read Q2 2025 data
indego1 <- read_csv(here("indego-trips-2025-q2.csv"))
```

Examining the data structure

```{r explore_data}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# How many trips?
cat("Total trips in Q2 2025:", nrow(indego1), "\n")

# Date range
cat("Date range:", 
    min(mdy_hm(indego1$start_time)), "to", 
    max(mdy_hm(indego1$start_time)), "\n")

# How many unique stations?
cat("Unique start stations:", length(unique(indego1$start_station)), "\n")%>%
  kable()

# Trip types
table(indego1$trip_route_category)%>%
  kable()

# Passholder types
table(indego1$passholder_type) %>%
  kable()

# Bike types
table(indego1$bike_type)%>%
  kable()
```

Aggregating trips into hourly bins.

```{r create-time-bins}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Clean up the dates and add useful time columns
indego1 <- indego1 %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse datetime
    start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time),
    end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time),
    
    # Create hourly bins
    interval601 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"),
    
    # Extract time features
    week = week(interval601),
    month = month(interval601, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval601, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval601),
    date = as.Date(interval601),
    
    # Create useful indicators
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )

# Look at temporal features
head(indego1 %>% dplyr::select(start_datetime, interval601, week, dotw, hour, weekend))
```

Here, we look at trips over time and then plot it for visual cues.

```{r trips_over_time}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Daily trip counts
daily_trips1 <- indego1 %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(trips = n())

# Plot daily trip counts with a trend line
ggplot(daily_trips1, aes(x = date, y = trips)) +
  geom_line(color = "purple", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q2 2025",
    subtitle = "Spring demand patterns in Philadelphia",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Daily Trips",
    caption = "Source: Indego bike share"
  ) +
  plotTheme1
```

Analyzing dip in the middle of may - as observed in the red dashed line that shows an overall trend of the data.

```{r abc}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

#choosing May 15 as the day of this dip 
dip_may1 <- daily_trips1 %>% filter(date == "2025-05-15")

# Compute the average Thursday ridership, excluding May 15 to use as a baseline comparison
typical_dipmay1 <- indego1 %>%
  filter(dotw == "Thu", date != "2025-05-15") %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarize(trips = n()) %>%
  summarize(avg_thursday = mean(trips))

# Display the May 15 ridership and the typical Thursday average as formatted tables
print(dip_may1)%>%
  kable()
print(typical_dipmay1)%>%
  kable()

```

This chunk computes average trips per hour separately for weekdays and weekends, then plots them as lines to highlight the contrast between commute-driven peaks on weekdays and flatter midday patterns on weekends.

```{r hourly_patterns}
#| message: false
#| warning: false 


# Compute average trips per hour for weekdays vs. weekends
hourly_patterns1 <- indego1 %>%
  group_by(hour, weekend) %>%
  summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>%
  mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday"))


# Plot weekday vs. weekend hourly ridership as lines to reveal commute peak patterns
ggplot(hourly_patterns1, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "purple", "Weekend" = "blue")) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns",
    subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays",
    x = "Hour of Day",
    y = "Average Trips per Hour",
    color = "Day Type"
  ) +
  plotTheme1
```

This chunk identifies and displays the 20 busiest Indego origin stations by total trip departures in a styled table.

```{r top_stations}
#| message: false
#| warning: false 

# Most popular origin stations
top_stations1 <- indego1 %>%
  count(start_station, start_lat, start_lon, name = "trips") %>%
  arrange(desc(trips)) %>%
  head(20)

# Display the top 20 stations as a formatted table with comma-separated trip counts
kable(top_stations1, 
      caption = "Top 20 Indego Stations by Trip Origins",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

This chunk pulls 2022 ACS census tract data for Philadelphia with geometry, renames the raw variable codes to readable names, and derives two new percentage variables (transit commute share and percent white) before reprojecting to WGS84 for spatial joining with the bike trip coordinates.

```{r load_census}
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# Get Philadelphia census tracts
philly_census1 <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    "B01003_001",  # Total population
    "B19013_001",  # Median household income
    "B08301_001",  # Total commuters
    "B08301_010",  # Commute by transit
    "B02001_002",  # White alone
    "B25077_001"   # Median home value
  ),
  state = "PA",
  county = "Philadelphia",
  year = 2022,
  geometry = TRUE,
  output = "wide"
) %>%
  rename(
    Total_Pop1 = B01003_001E,
    Med_Inc1 = B19013_001E,
    Total_Commuters1 = B08301_001E,
    Transit_Commuters1 = B08301_010E,
    White_Pop1 = B02001_002E,
    Med_Home_Value1 = B25077_001E
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Percent_Taking_Transit1 = (Transit_Commuters1 / Total_Commuters1) * 100,
    Percent_White1 = (White_Pop1 / Total_Pop1) * 100
  ) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)  #transforming to WGS84 for lat/lon matching, currently in NAD83

```

This chunk maps Philadelphia census tracts shaded by median household income, with Indego bike share trip origin points overlaid in red to visually assess whether ridership concentrates in higher- or lower-income neighborhoods.

```{r map_1philly}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Map median income
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Med_Inc1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  # Stations 
  geom_point(
    data = indego1,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  mapTheme1
```

This chunk spatially joins each Indego station to its Philadelphia census tract to attach demographic variables, merges those demographics back onto the full trip dataset, then maps all stations flagged by whether they successfully matched to census data, highlighting unmatched stations in red to diagnose coverage gaps in non-residential tracts.

```{r join_census_to_stations}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create sf object for stations
stations_sf1 <- indego1 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatial join to get census tract for each station
stations_census1 <- st_join(stations_sf1, philly_census1, left = TRUE) %>%
  st_drop_geometry()

# Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts.
stations_for_map1 <- indego1 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc1))

# Add back to trip data
indego_census1 <- indego1 %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% 
      dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1, Percent_Taking_Transit1, 
             Percent_White1, Total_Pop1),
    by = "start_station"
  )


# Prepare data for visualization
stations_for_map1 <- indego1 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc1))

# Create the map showing problem stations
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Med_Inc1), color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar,
    na.value = "grey90"
  ) +
  # Stations with census data (small grey dots)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map1 %>% filter(has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map1 %>% filter(!has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)",
    caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts"
  ) +
  mapTheme1
```

This chunk filters the trip data to only stations that successfully matched census tracts, then joins the demographic variables onto those trips to produce a clean dataset ready for analysis.

```{r def}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Identify which stations to keep
valid_stations1 <- stations_census1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(Med_Inc1)) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Filter trip data to valid stations only
indego_census1 <- indego1 %>%
  filter(start_station %in% valid_stations1) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% 
      dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1, Percent_Taking_Transit1, 
             Percent_White1, Total_Pop1),
    by = "start_station"
  )

```

This chunk pulls hourly weather data from Philadelphia International Airport for Q2 2025, cleans and standardizes the temperature, precipitation, and wind speed fields, fills any missing hours by carrying forward the last known value, then prints a summary table of the three weather variables.

```{r get_weather}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Get weather from Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL)
# This covers Q2 2025: Apr 01 - June 31
weather_data1 <- riem_measures(
  station = "PHL",  # Philadelphia International Airport
  date_start = "2025-04-01",
  date_end = "2025-06-30"
)

# Process weather data
weather_processed1 <- weather_data1 %>%
  mutate(
    interval601 = floor_date(valid, unit = "hour"),
    Temperature1 = tmpf,  # Temperature in Fahrenheit
    Precipitation1 = ifelse(is.na(p01i), 0, p01i),  # Hourly precip in inches
    Wind_Speed1 = sknt  # Wind speed in knots
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(interval601, Temperature1, Precipitation1, Wind_Speed1) %>%
  distinct(interval601, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Check for missing hours and interpolate if needed
weather_complete1 <- weather_processed1 %>%
  complete(interval601 = seq(min(interval601), max(interval601), by = "hour")) %>%
  fill(Temperature1, Precipitation1, Wind_Speed1, .direction = "down")

# Look at the weather
summary(weather_complete1 %>% dplyr::select(Temperature1, Precipitation1, Wind_Speed1))%>%
  kable()
```

This chunk visualizes hourly Philadelphia temperatures across Q2 2025 with a smoothed trend line to show the gradual warming progression from spring into early summer.

```{r visualize-weather}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Plot hourly temperature over Q2 2025 with a smoothed trend line
ggplot(weather_complete1, aes(x = interval601, y = Temperature1)) +
  geom_line(color = "yellow", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "purple") +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Temperature - Q2 2025",
    subtitle = "Spring to early summer transition",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Temperature (°F)"
  ) +
  plotTheme1
```

This chunk aggregates trip data into a station-hour panel by counting departures for each station and hour combination while retaining demographic variables, then prints basic dimension checks and a histogram to inspect the distribution of hourly trip counts per station.

```{r aggregate-trips}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Count trips by station-hour
trips_panel1 <- indego_census1 %>%
  group_by(interval601, start_station, start_lat, start_lon,
           Med_Inc1, Percent_Taking_Transit1, Percent_White1, Total_Pop1) %>%
  summarize(Trip_Count1 = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

# How many station-hour observations?
nrow(trips_panel1)

# How many unique stations?
length(unique(trips_panel1$start_station))

# How many unique hours?
length(unique(trips_panel1$interval601))

#histgram of analysis
hist(trips_panel1$Trip_Count1)
```

This chunk builds a complete station-hour panel by expanding all station and hour combinations, filling missing trip counts with zero, and rejoining station-level demographic attributes, ensuring every station has an observation for every hour even when no trips occurred.

```{r complete-panel}
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# Calculate expected panel size
n_stations1 <- length(unique(trips_panel1$start_station))
n_hours1 <- length(unique(trips_panel1$interval601))
expected_rows1 <- n_stations1 * n_hours1

cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows1, big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel1), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Missing rows:", format(expected_rows1 - nrow(trips_panel1), big.mark = ","), "\n")

# Create complete panel
study_panel1 <- expand_grid(
  interval601 = unique(trips_panel1$interval601),
  start_station = unique(trips_panel1$start_station)
) %>%
  # Join trip counts
  left_join(trips_panel1 %>% 
              dplyr::select(interval601, start_station, Trip_Count1),
            by = c("interval601", "start_station")) %>%
  # Replace NA trip counts with 0
  mutate(Trip_Count1 = replace_na(Trip_Count1, 0))

# histogram of analysis
hist(study_panel1$Trip_Count1)

# Fill in station attributes (they're the same for all hours)
station_attributes1 <- trips_panel1 %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  summarize(
    start_lat = first(start_lat),
    start_lon = first(start_lon),
    Med_Inc1 = first(Med_Inc1),
    Percent_Taking_Transit1 = first(Percent_Taking_Transit1),
    Percent_White1 = first(Percent_White1),
    Total_Pop1 = first(Total_Pop1)
  )


study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  left_join(station_attributes1, by = "start_station")


# Verify we have complete panel
cat("Complete panel rows:", format(nrow(study_panel1), big.mark = ","), "\n")
```

This chunk extracts week, month, day of week, hour, and date from the timestamp, then flags each observation as weekend or rush hour to create time-based features for demand prediction.

```{r add_time_features}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Engineer time-based features from the interval timestamp for use as model predictors
study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  mutate(
    week = week(interval601),
    month = month(interval601, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval601, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval601),
    date = as.Date(interval601),
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )


```

This chunk joins hourly weather conditions onto the study panel by timestamp, then prints a summary to check for any missing values in the key trip count and weather fields.

```{r join_weather}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  left_join(weather_complete1, by = "interval601")


# Check for missing values
summary(study_panel1 %>% dplyr::select(Trip_Count1, Temperature1, Precipitation1))
```

This chunk creates lagged trip count features at 1, 2, 3, 12, and 24 hour intervals within each station, then drops the initial rows where lags cannot be computed to produce a complete dataset ready for modeling.

```{r create_lags}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Sort by station and time
study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  arrange(start_station, interval601)

# Create lag variables WITHIN each station
study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  mutate(
    lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count1, 1),
    lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count1, 2),
    lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count1, 3),
    lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count1, 12),
    lag1day = lag(Trip_Count1, 24)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station)
study_panel_complete1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(lag1day))

cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete1), big.mark = ","), "\n")
```

This chunk plots one week of trip counts at a single station alongside its 1-hour and 24-hour lagged values to visually confirm that past demand tracks closely with current demand, validating the lag features as useful predictors.

```{r lag_correlations}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Sample one station to visualize
example_station1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>%
  head(168)  # One week

# Plot actual vs lagged demand
ggplot(example_station1, aes(x = interval601)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count1, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Current" = "red",
    "1 Hour Ago" = "lightblue",
    "24 Hours Ago" = "yellow"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station",
    subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand",
    x = "Date-Time",
    y = "Trip Count",
    color = "Time Period"
  ) +
  plotTheme1

```

This chunk splits the panel into train and test sets by week, retaining only stations with trips in both periods to prevent data leakage, then prints observation counts and date ranges to confirm the split.

```{r temporal-split}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Split by week
# Q2 has weeks 14-26 (Apr - Jun)
# Train on weeks 14-22(Apr 1 - early June)
# Test on weeks 23-26 (rest of June)

# Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods?
early_stations1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week < 23) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count1 > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)

late_stations1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week >= 23) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count1 > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods
common_stations1 <- intersect(early_stations1, late_stations1)


# Filter panel to only common stations
study_panel_complete1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(start_station %in% common_stations1)

# NOW create train/test split
train1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week < 23)

test1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week >= 23)

cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train1), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(test1), big.mark = ","), "\n")
cat("Training date range:", min(train1$date), "to", max(train1$date), "\n")
cat("Testing date range:", min(test1$date), "to", max(test1$date), "\n")



study_panel_complete1 %>%
  distinct(week) %>%
  arrange(week)

```

This chunk fits a baseline linear regression predicting hourly trip counts using hour of day, day of week, temperature, and precipitation, with Monday as the reference category for day of week dummy coding.

```{r model1}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding
train1 <- train1 %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

# Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables)
contrasts(train1$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

# Now run the model
model11 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1,
  data = train1
)

summary(model11)
```

This chunk extends the baseline model by adding 1-hour, 3-hour, and 24-hour lagged trip counts as predictors, testing whether recent and same-time-yesterday demand improves forecast accuracy beyond time and weather alone.

```{r model2}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Add lag features to the baseline model and check results
model21 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day,
  data = train1
)

summary(model21)
```

This chunk extends model 2 by adding neighborhood demographic variables to test whether the census tract characteristics of a station's location explain additional variation in hourly trip counts.

```{r model3}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Add neighborhood demographics to see if they improve predictions
model31 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1,
  data = train1
)

summary(model31)
```

Extends model 3 by adding station fixed effects to absorb time-invariant differences across locations, then prints only R-squared metrics since the full summary would be unwieldy with hundreds of station dummies.

```{r model4}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Add station fixed effects to account for location-specific differences
model41 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station),
  data = train1
)

# Summary too long with all station dummies, just show key metrics
cat("Model 4 R-squared:", summary(model41)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Model 4 Adj R-squared:", summary(model41)$adj.r.squared, "\n")
```

This chunk extends model 4 by adding rush hour and month fixed effects plus a rush hour and weekend interaction term, capturing the intuition that morning and evening peaks behave differently on weekdays versus weekends.

```{r model5}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Add rush hour, month, and a rush hour-weekend interaction term
model51 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + rush_hour + as.factor(month) +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) +
    rush_hour * weekend,  # Rush hour effects different on weekends
  data = train1
)

cat("Model 5 R-squared:", summary(model51)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Model 5 Adj R-squared:", summary(model51)$adj.r.squared, "\n")
```

This chunk generates test set predictions from all five models, computes MAE for each, and displays the results as both a formatted table and a bar chart to directly compare how much each added feature reduced prediction error.

```{r calculate_mae}
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# Get predictions on test set

# Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding
test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

# Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables)
contrasts(test1$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    pred11 = predict(model11, newdata = test1),
    pred21 = predict(model21, newdata = test1),
    pred31 = predict(model31, newdata = test1),
    pred41 = predict(model41, newdata = test1),
    pred51 = predict(model51, newdata = test1)
  )

# Calculate MAE for each model
mae_results1 <- data.frame(
  Model = c(
    "1. Time + Weather",
    "2. + Temporal Lags",
    "3. + Demographics",
    "4. + Station FE",
    "5. + Rush Hour Interaction"
  ),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred11), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred21), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred31), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred41), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred51), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

kable(mae_results1, 
      digits = 3,
      caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)",
      col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

This chunk visualizes Model Comparison

```{r compare_models}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Bar chart comparing MAE across all models
ggplot(mae_results1, aes(x = reorder(Model, -MAE), y = MAE)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(MAE, 3)), vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Model Performance Comparison",
    subtitle = "Lower MAE = Better Predictions",
    x = "Model",
    y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)"
  ) +
  plotTheme1 +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

This chunk computes residuals and labels each observation by time of day, then plots observed versus predicted trip counts in a faceted scatter plot split by time period and weekend status to diagnose where model 4 over- or underpredicts.

```{r obs_vs_pred}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Calculate errors and label each observation by time of day
test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    error = Trip_Count1 - pred41,
    abs_error = abs(error),
    time_of_day = case_when(
      hour < 7 ~ "Overnight",
      hour >= 7 & hour < 10 ~ "AM Rush",
      hour >= 10 & hour < 15 ~ "Mid-Day",
      hour >= 15 & hour <= 18 ~ "PM Rush",
      hour > 18 ~ "Evening"
    )
  )

# Scatter plot by time and day type
ggplot(test1, aes(x = Trip_Count1, y = pred41)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkgreen") +
  facet_grid(weekend ~ time_of_day) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs. Predicted Bike Trips",
    subtitle = "Model 4 performance by time period",
    x = "Observed Trips",
    y = "Predicted Trips",
    caption = "Red line = perfect predictions; Green line = actual model fit"
  ) +
  plotTheme1
```

This chunk computes per-station MAE and average demand from the test set, then maps both side by side across Philadelphia to reveal whether prediction errors cluster spatially, particularly in high-demand areas like Center City.

```{r spatial_errors}
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# Calculate MAE by station
station_errors <- test1 %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_demand1 = mean(Trip_Count1, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

## Create Two Maps Side-by-Side with Proper Legends

# Calculate station errors
station_errors1 <- test1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(pred41)) %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE = mean(abs(Trip_Count1 - pred41), na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_demand1 = mean(Trip_Count1, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))


# Map 1: Prediction Errors
x1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  geom_point(
    data = station_errors1,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = MAE),
    size = 3.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis(
    option = "plasma",
    name = "MAE (trips)",
    direction = -1,
    breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5),
    labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Prediction Errors") +
  mapTheme1 +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_colorbar(
    barwidth = 12,
    barheight = 1,
    title.position = "top",
    title.hjust = 0.5
  ))

# Map 2: Average Demand  
x2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  geom_point(
    data = station_errors1,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = avg_demand1),
    size = 3.5,
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Avg Demand (trips/hour)",
    direction = -1,
    breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5),
    labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5", "2.0", "2.5")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Average Demand") +
  mapTheme1 +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  guides(color = guide_colorbar(
    barwidth = 12,
    barheight = 1,
    title.position = "top",
    title.hjust = 0.5
  ))

# Combine
(x1 | x2)

```

This chunk computes MAE by time of day and weekday versus weekend, then plots them as a grouped bar chart to identify which time periods the model struggles to predict most accurately.

```{r temporal_errors}
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# MAE by time of day and day type
temporal_errors1 <- test1 %>%
  group_by(time_of_day, weekend) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday"))

# Bar chart of errors by time of day split by weekday vs weekend
ggplot(temporal_errors1, aes(x = time_of_day, y = MAE, fill = day_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) +
  labs(
    title = "Prediction Errors by Time Period",
    subtitle = "When is the model struggling most?",
    x = "Time of Day",
    y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)",
    fill = "Day Type"
  ) +
  plotTheme1 +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

This chunk joins demographic attributes onto station-level errors and plots MAE against median income, transit usage, and racial composition to assess whether the model predicts less accurately in certain types of neighborhoods.

```{r errors_demographics}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Join demographic data to station errors
station_errors_demo1 <- station_errors1 %>%
  left_join(
    station_attributes1 %>% dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1, Percent_Taking_Transit1, Percent_White1),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Med_Inc1))

# Create plots
p11 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo1, aes(x = Med_Inc1, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Median Income", x = "Median Income", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme1

p21 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo1, aes(x = Percent_Taking_Transit1, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Transit Usage", x = "% Taking Transit", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme1

p31 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo1, aes(x = Percent_White1, y = MAE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Errors vs. Race", x = "% White", y = "MAE") +
  plotTheme1

(p11 | p21) / (p31 | plot_spacer())
```

## Comparison with Q1 Data

[**How do MAE values compare? Why might they differ?**]{.underline}

*MAE for Q1 below:*

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Compared to Q2, the MAE for Q1 is consistently lower across all models, suggesting that Q1 data is more predictable. Q2 shows greater demand variability because overall ridership increases in the second quarter compared to the first. With increased ridership, patterns change, making it more difficult to predict demand accurately.

[**Are temporal patterns different (e.g., summer vs. winter?**]{.underline}

Temporal patterns are definitely different in the spring vs the winter. First, the spring has higher usage than the winter. Another, from early days of Q1 to late Q1 the ridership kept increasing, in a steeper slope. However, for this one the ridership increases slower with a more flattened direction.

[**Which features are most important in your quarter?**]{.underline}

The most important feature is the temporal lag, where the MAE dropped from 0.835 to 0.690, a 0.145 point decrease, the most substantive change across all models. Therefore, temporal lag is the most important feature in this case.

# Part 2: Error Analysis

## 1. Create error maps

We pick Model 4, with the lowest MAE for the error analysis. This contains all features included other than the rush hour effect. The rush hour increased our MAE therefore, Model 4 was chosen. The error map below showcases where the errors are, and attempts to explain why.

```{r errorabc}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

#station error 
station_errors1 <- test1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(pred41)) %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE       = mean(abs(Trip_Count1 - pred41), na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_trips = mean(Trip_Count1, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_obs     = n(),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

#Convert stations to spatial feature
station_errors_sf1 <- station_errors1 %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatial join-  each station to its census tract
station_errors_tract1 <- st_join(
  station_errors_sf1,
  philly_census1 %>% select(GEOID, geometry),   # philly_census already in 4326
  join = st_within
)

#aggregate it to tract level
tract_errors1 <- station_errors_tract1 %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(GEOID) %>%
  summarize(
    tract_MAE1      = weighted.mean(MAE, w = n_obs, na.rm = TRUE),
    tract_demand1   = mean(avg_trips, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_stations1     = n(),
    .groups        = "drop"
  )

#join to tract geometry
philly_tract_errors1 <- philly_census1 %>%
  left_join(tract_errors1, by = "GEOID")

#Map 1 tract level error
ahm1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(
    data  = philly_tract_errors1,
    aes(fill = tract_MAE1),
    color = "white", linewidth = 0.02
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option   = "plasma",
    name     = "Weighted\nMAE (trips)",
    direction = 1,
    na.value = "grey85"
  ) +
  # Overlay station dots for reference
  geom_point(
    data  = station_errors,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "white", size = 0.008, alpha = 0.05
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Prediction Error by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Model 4, weighted mean MAE per tract (test set, weeks 23–26)",
    caption  = "Grey tracts have no Indego stations"
  ) +
  mapTheme1


#mapping neighborhoods by residual errors
res1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(residual = Trip_Count1 - pred41)
  
# Calculate mean residual per station
station_residuals1 <- res1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(pred41)) %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    mean_residual = mean(residual, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_obs         = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

# Convert stations to spatial object
station_residuals_sf1 <- station_residuals1 %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatially join stations to census tracts
station_residuals_tract1 <- st_join(
  station_residuals_sf1,
  philly_census1 %>% dplyr::select(GEOID, geometry),
  join = st_within
)

# Aggregate residuals to tract level
tract_residuals1 <- station_residuals_tract1 %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(GEOID) %>%
  summarize(
    tract_mean_residual = weighted.mean(mean_residual, w = n_obs, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_stations          = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Join residuals back to tract geometry for mapping
philly_tract_residuals1 <- philly_census1 %>%
  left_join(tract_residuals1, by = "GEOID")


#mapping it
ahm2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(
    data  = philly_tract_residuals1,
    aes(fill = tract_mean_residual),
    color = "white", linewidth = 0.02
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "darkred",     # red  = overpredicted (model too high)
    high     = "darkblue",  # blue = underpredicted (model too low)
    midpoint = 0,
    name     = "Mean\nResidual\n(trips)",
    na.value = "grey85"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Where Does Our Model Struggle?",
    subtitle = "Mean Signed Residual by Census Tract (test set, weeks 23–26)",
    caption  = "Blue = model underestimates demand | Red = model overestimates demand\nGrey tracts have no Indego stations"
  ) +
  mapTheme1


(ahm1)
```

```{r errordefg}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# map of errors
(ahm2)

```

[**Identify neighborhoods with high errors**]{.underline}

As we can observe in the map, the errors are mostly concentrated around the Center City area and periphery. , which is also where the model seemingly under-predicts.

[**Hypothesize why (missing features? different demand patterns?)**]{.underline}

This happens primarily because of high demand fluctuation in this region. There may be unobserved factors contributing to this, or other missing spatial features that need to be considered in our model. Center City, as the name suggests, consists of the urban core of the Philadelphia region, leading to travel patterns driven by a wide range of trip purposes. As a result, it is not always certain what the demand pattern in this region is going to be.

## 2. Temporal Error

[**When are errors highest?**]{.underline}

```{r xyz}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| 
# Calculate MAE by hour, day of week, and day type
temporal_errors2 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday")) %>%
  group_by(dotw, hour, day_type) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Plot errors by hour by day of week
ggplot(temporal_errors2, aes(x = hour, y = MAE, fill = hour)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Hour") +
  facet_wrap(~ dotw, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Prediction Errors by Hour and Day of Week",
    subtitle = "When are errors highest?",
    x = "Hour of Day",
    y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)",
    fill = "Hour"
  ) +
  plotTheme1 +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, size = 7))
```

From the graph above, we can identify the hours with the most errors. Here, just past 3 PM, corresponding with the previously created bar graph grouped by weekday versus weekend and time of day, the PM rush hour was found to be the most difficult to predict, with the highest MAE. Tuesday PM rush hour, around 17:00 or 5:00 PM, seems to have the greatest MAE.

```{r popular error}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Pull and display the 5 worst-performing hour-day combinations
top_error <- temporal_errors2 %>%
  arrange(desc(MAE)) %>%
  head(5) %>%
  dplyr::select(dotw, hour, day_type, MAE)

kable(top_error,
      digits = 3,
      caption = "Top 5 Highest Error Hour-Day Combinations",
      col.names = c("Day of Week", "Hour", "Day Type", "Error"),
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

The table here goes onto prove the suspicion, where 5 PM for most weekdays have high errors, but Tuesday specifically has the highest.

```{r lmno}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Calculate mean signed residuals by hour, day of week, and day type
temporal_errors3 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday"),
    residual = Trip_Count1 - pred41
  ) %>%
  group_by(dotw, hour, day_type) %>%
  summarize(
    mean_residual = mean(residual, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Plot residuals by hour faceted by day to show over/underprediction patterns
ggplot(temporal_errors3, aes(x = hour, y = mean_residual, fill = hour)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Hour") +
  facet_wrap(~ dotw, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Residuals by Hour and Day of Week",
    subtitle = "Positive = underpredicted | Negative = overpredicted",
    x = "Hour of Day",
    y = "Mean Residual (trips)",
    fill = "Hour"
  ) +
  plotTheme1 +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, size = 7))

```

```{r pqr}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Pull the 5 most underpredicted hour-day combinations
top_error3 <- temporal_errors3 %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_residual)) %>%
  head(5)

# Pull the 5 most overpredicted hour-day combinations
top_error4 <- temporal_errors3 %>%
  arrange(mean_residual) %>%
  head(5)

kable(top_error3,
      digits = 3,
      caption = "Top 5 Highest Residual Hour-Day Combinations",
      col.names = c("Day of Week", "Hour", "Day Type", "Mean Residual"),
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

kable(top_error4,
      digits = 3,
      caption = "Top 5 Lowest Residual Hour-Day Combinations",
      col.names = c("Day of Week", "Hour", "Day Type", "Mean Residual"),
      format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

[**Do certain hours/days have systematic under/over-prediction?**]{.underline}

Monday and Tuesday during evening rush hour at 5 PM, the model tends to underpredict demand. Similarly, Wednesday at 8 AM shows the highest underprediction, and Monday at 8 AM also exhibits underprediction. Interestingly, on Friday nights at 11 PM, the model also underpredicts demand.

Conversely, the model overpredicts on Saturday and Sunday mornings, as well as during the 5 PM rush hour on those days.

[**Are there seasonal patterns?**]{.underline}

For quarter 1 the patterns are as such presented below:

![](data:image/png;base64,)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

\
Comparing it with the one for the second quarter, the spatial patterns are both similar and different. For underprediction, Tuesday and Monday remain constant in the top five. However, interestingly, Saturday at 1 PM is also underpredicted. This may perhaps be attributed to winter weekend afternoons being warmer and more favorable. Thursday at 5 PM is also underpredicted in the first quarter, which is not observed in the second-quarter data.

For overprediction, Saturday at 8 AM and Saturday at 5 PM remain constant. However, Thursday at 8 PM seems to be overpredicted in Quarter 1, which is absent from Quarter 2.

## 3. Demographic Patterns

```{r map_philly}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Map median income
emale <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Med_Inc1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  mapTheme1

rsp <- ahm1


```

```{r vwxn}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Map total population
bmale <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Total_Pop1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Total\nPopulation",
    labels
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Population by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  mapTheme1

```

```{r opq}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Map commuters
cmale <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Total_Commuters1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Total\nCommuters",
    labels 
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Total Commuters by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  mapTheme1


```

```{r shu}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Map transit commuters
dmale <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Transit_Commuters1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Total\nTransit\nCommuters",
    labels
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Total Transit Commuters by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  mapTheme1


```

```{r bhan}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Map white popultion share
eemale <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = White_Pop1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Total\nWhite\nPopulation",
    labels
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Total White Population by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  mapTheme1

```

```{r ga}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Map median home value
fmale <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Med_Home_Value1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nHome\nValue",
    labels = scales::dollar
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Home Value by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  mapTheme1


```

```{r sat}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

combined <- wrap_plots(emale, bmale, cmale, dmale, eemale, fmale, ahm1, ncol = 2)

ggsave("combined_maps.png", combined, width = 22, height = 28, dpi = 200)

knitr::include_graphics("combined_maps.png")
```

[**Relate errors to census characteristics - Are certain communities systematically harder to predict- What are the equity implications?**]{.underline}

Among the considered census characteristics, the ones that seem to visually coincide with the errors are median home value and, consequently, median household income as well. This gives us some direction in understanding how unpredictable the demand patterns are in these considerably high-income areas, which coincide with Center City, Philadelphia’s central business district. Errors are likely to be concentrated here because these areas may be sensitive to many spatial, economic, and temporal factors.

# Part 3: Feature Engineering

```{r yalama}

#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Load and filter transit stops
transit <- st_read("Transit_Stops_(Spring_2026).shp") %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)

transit_data <- transit %>%
  filter(Line %in% c("MFL", "BSL", "K",
                     "T1", "T2", "T3", "T4", "T5",
                     "D1", "D2",
                     "G1", "M1",
                     "204", "206", "310", "311", "312", "316"))

# Filter station_attributes ONCE and create sf object 
station_attributes1 <- station_attributes1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

station_attributes_sf1 <- station_attributes1 %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# KNN helper function 
knn_distance <- function(dist_mat, k) {
  apply(dist_mat, 1, function(d) mean(as.numeric(sort(d)[1:k])))
}

# Transit KNN distance 
transit_distance <- st_distance(station_attributes_sf1, transit_data)

station_attributes1 <- station_attributes1 %>%
  mutate(transit_knn3 = knn_distance(transit_distance, k = 3))

# Commercial corridor KNN distance 
commercial_corridor <- st_read("19933e44-ece7-40b5-b8a5-5e70f80e4b9a2020329-1-e5xsp1.l33b7.shp") %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)

comcor_distance_matrix <- st_distance(station_attributes_sf1, commercial_corridor)

station_attributes1 <- station_attributes1 %>%
  mutate(comcor_knn3 = knn_distance(comcor_distance_matrix, k = 3))

# Drop any existing KNN columns before joining to avoid .x/.y conflicts
study_panel_complete1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  select(-any_of(c("transit_knn3", "comcor_knn3",
                   "transit_knn3.x", "transit_knn3.y",
                   "comcor_knn3.x", "comcor_knn3.y")))

# Join KNN features cleanly
study_panel_complete1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  left_join(
    station_attributes1 %>% dplyr::select(start_station, transit_knn3, comcor_knn3),
    by = "start_station"
  )

# Define public holidays and add binary indicator
public_holidays <- as.Date(c(
  "2025-04-18",  # Good Friday
  "2025-05-26",  # Memorial Day
  "2025-06-19"   # Juneteenth
))

study_panel_complete1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  mutate(is_holiday = as.integer(as.Date(date) %in% public_holidays))

# Single clean train/test split 
train1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week < 23) %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))
contrasts(train1$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

test1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week >= 23) %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))
contrasts(test1$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

# Models 6, 7, 8, 9

# Model 4 + distance to nearest transit stops
model61 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) + transit_knn3,
  data = train1
)
cat("Model 6 R-squared:", summary(model61)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Model 6 Adj R-squared:", summary(model61)$adj.r.squared, "\n")

# Model 4 + distance to nearest commercial corridors
model71 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) + comcor_knn3,
  data = train1
)
cat("Model 7 R-squared:", summary(model71)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Model 7 Adj R-squared:", summary(model71)$adj.r.squared, "\n")

# Model 4 + distance to nearest transit stops and commercial corridors
model81 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) + transit_knn3 + comcor_knn3,
  data = train1
)
cat("Model 8 R-squared:", summary(model81)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Model 8 Adj R-squared:", summary(model81)$adj.r.squared, "\n")

# Model 9: Full spatial features + public holiday indicator
model91 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) + transit_knn3 + comcor_knn3 + is_holiday,
  data = train1
)
cat("Model 9 R-squared:", summary(model91)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Model 9 Adj R-squared:", summary(model91)$adj.r.squared, "\n")

# Model 4 + public holiday indicator
model101 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) + is_holiday,
  data = train1
)
cat("Model 10 R-squared:", summary(model101)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Model 9 Adj R-squared:", summary(model101)$adj.r.squared, "\n")


# Predictions on test set
test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    pred11 = predict(model11, newdata = test1),
    pred21 = predict(model21, newdata = test1),
    pred31 = predict(model31, newdata = test1),
    pred41 = predict(model41, newdata = test1),
    pred51 = predict(model51, newdata = test1),
    pred61 = predict(model61, newdata = test1),
    pred71 = predict(model71, newdata = test1),
    pred81 = predict(model81, newdata = test1),
    pred91 = predict(model91, newdata = test1),
    pred101 = predict(model101, newdata = test1)
  )

# MAE table
mae_results1 <- data.frame(
  Model = c(
    "1. Time + Weather",
    "2. + Temporal Lags",
    "3. + Demographics",
    "4. + Station FE",
    "5. + Rush Hour Interaction",
    "6. + k nearest transit",
    "7. + k nearest commercial corridor",
    "8. + k nearest transit and comcor",
    "9. + k nearest transit and comcor + Holiday",
    "10. + Only Holiday"
  ),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred11), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred21), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred31), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred41), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred51), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred61), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred71), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred81), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred91), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred101), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

kable(mae_results1,
      digits = 5,
      caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)",
      col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))


```

I chose distance to the nearest transit stop, including only train, regional rail, and trolley stops. I chose this because commuter needs are mostly based on the need to reach certain locations, and proximity to these transit facilities would have been a decent indicator. However, this variable was added to Model 4, meaning the model already included spatial fixed effects, which perhaps accounted for many of these factors, and the model therefore did not improve much. Surprisingly, proximity to commercial corridors also had no effect on model fit, perhaps giving us insight into the fact that bike stations may already be planned in proximity to these locations as well. Adding holidays deteriorated the model a little, perhaps because only three dates are official holidays for this period, April 3 - Good Friday, May 25, Memorial Day, June 19, Juneteenth. However since the data is sparse the model does not seem to improve the data.

\

```{r poisson}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Rerun model 9 as a Poisson regression to better handle count data with all added factors
model_poisson1 <- glm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) + transit_knn3 +comcor_knn3 + is_holiday,
  data = train1,
  family = poisson
)

test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(pred_poisson1 = predict(model_poisson1, newdata = test1, type = "response"))

mae_poisson1 <- mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred_poisson1), na.rm = TRUE)
cat("Poisson MAE:", mae_poisson1, "\n")
cat("Model 8 MAE:", mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred81), na.rm = TRUE), "\n")

# Rerun model 9 as a Poisson regression to better handle count data nly same as model 4
model_poisson2 <- glm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station),
  data = train1,
  family = poisson
)

```

```{r lastpoisson}
#| message: false
#| warning: false


# Predictions on test set
test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    pred11 = predict(model11, newdata = test1),
    pred21 = predict(model21, newdata = test1),
    pred31 = predict(model31, newdata = test1),
    pred41 = predict(model41, newdata = test1),
    pred51 = predict(model51, newdata = test1),
    pred61 = predict(model61, newdata = test1),
    pred71 = predict(model71, newdata = test1),
    pred81 = predict(model81, newdata = test1),
    pred91 = predict(model91, newdata = test1),
    pred101 = predict(model101, newdata = test1),
    pred_poisson1 = predict(model_poisson1, newdata = test1, type = "response"),
    pred_poisson2 = predict(model_poisson2, newdata = test1, type = "response")
  )

# MAE table
mae_results1 <- data.frame(
  Model = c(
    "1. Time + Weather",
    "2. + Temporal Lags",
    "3. + Demographics",
    "4. + Station FE",
    "5. + Rush Hour Interaction",
    "6. + k nearest transit",
    "7. + k nearest commercial corridor",
    "8. + k nearest transit and comcor",
    "9. + holiday",
    "10. model 4 + holiday only",
    "11. Poisson Model for 9.",
    "12. Poisson on Model 4"
    
  ),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred11), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred21), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred31), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred41), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred51), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred61), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred71), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred81), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred91), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred101), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred_poisson1), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred_poisson2), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

kable(mae_results1,
      digits = 5,
      caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)",
      col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```

After conducting the Poisson model on Model 4, we do see a change in the MAE, which is now at the lowest point of 0.661, compared to all the other models previously created.

```{r poisson2_residuals}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Compute residuals for Poisson Model 2
test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    resid_poisson2 = Trip_Count1 - pred_poisson2
  )

# Station-level summary
station_resid_p2 <- test1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(pred_poisson2)) %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    mean_residual = mean(resid_poisson2, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_obs         = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

# Aggregate to census tract
station_resid_p2_sf <- station_resid_p2 %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

station_resid_p2_tract <- st_join(
  station_resid_p2_sf,
  philly_census1 %>% dplyr::select(GEOID, geometry),
  join = st_within
)

tract_resid_p2 <- station_resid_p2_tract %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(GEOID) %>%
  summarize(
    tract_mean_residual = weighted.mean(mean_residual, w = n_obs, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

philly_tract_resid_p2 <- philly_census1 %>%
  left_join(tract_resid_p2, by = "GEOID")

# Signed residuals map
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_tract_resid_p2, aes(fill = tract_mean_residual),
          color = "white", linewidth = 0.02) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "darkred",
    high     = "darkblue",
    midpoint = 0,
    name     = "Mean\nResidual\n(trips)",
    na.value = "grey85"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Poisson Model 2: Signed Residuals by Tract",
    subtitle = "Mean Signed Residual (test set, weeks 23–26)",
    caption  = "Blue = model underestimates demand | Red = model overestimates demand\nGrey tracts have no Indego stations"
  ) +
  mapTheme1
```

# Part 4: Critical Reflection

## Operational Implications

Compared to the the rest of the models the MAE of 0.661 of the poisson distribution is good enough for Compared to the rest of the models, the MAE of 0.661 for the Poisson distribution is good enough for implementation in this case. However, when considering the data from Quarter 1, which had a lower MAE in the range of 0.5 to 0.6, this performance is not good enough. In our case, the model seems to overpredict more than it underpredicts, and this may create problems for rebalancing, as more bikes than demanded may be circulated unnecessarily, causing financial stress for Indego. I would recommend implementing this system under strict precautions to avoid an oversupply of cycles.

## Equity Considerations

The prediction errors, as earlier mentioned, disproportionately affect central areas. However, as evidenced in the mean residual of the Poisson model above, the error is centered around Center City, with some areas in the western part of Philadelphia also affected. University City is also affected by overprediction; however, this is not an equity concern, but rather a managerial one. There is minimal underprediction. While there are some areas

## Model Limitations

The model has several important limitations that must be acknowledged. First, the weather data comes from a single airport station, and thus may not accurately capture the hyperlocal weather conditions across Philadelphia’s many different neighborhoods. Beyond this, there are many unobserved demand drivers, such as events, construction activity, or street-level urban conditions, that are completely absent from the model. This becomes more evident given that the spatial features added in Part 3, namely transit proximity, commercial corridors, and holidays, did not contribute much to improving predictive performance, perhaps suggesting that other important factors still remain uncaptured.

Further, the model relies heavily on station fixed effects, which limits its ability to generalize to new or hypothetical stations. Structurally as well, the fixed-coefficient framework appears to struggle with the more complex and volatile demand patterns of dense urban cores such as Center City, where the errors seem to be more concentrated. In addition, the lag structure adds another layer of limitation, because an error in one hour can carry forward and affect the lagged predictions in the following hours. Finally, the demographic analysis is conducted at the census tract level, which may hide more fine-grained inequities at the block or neighborhood scale or vs using fishnet, and thus may understate disparities in model performance for certain communities.

# AI Acknowledgment

I have leveraged AI for cleaning and debugging my codes, understamnding codes from class assignment, for it to explain certain codes I was unfamiliar with.
